title: Giant Spin Magnetization from Quantum Geometry in Altermagnets
authors: Neelanjan Chakraborti, Sudeep Kumar Ghosh, Snehasish Nandy
abstract: Altermagnets host spin-split band structures while exhibiting vanishing equilibrium spin magnetization, making field-induced responses a direct probe of their quantum geometry. A central question, in this regard, is which quantum-geometric mechanism can generate a linear spin magnetization in centrosymmetric systems. Here we develop a unified framework based on a generalized quantum geometric tensor that incorporates both momentum translations and spin rotations of Bloch states, and decompose spin magnetization into equilibrium, electric-field-driven, and magnetic-field-driven contributions. We show that inversion symmetry forbids the linear electric-field response in centrosymmetric systems, while $C_n T$ symmetry further suppresses the equilibrium contribution in altermagnets. Consequently, centrosymmetric altermagnets provide a particularly clean realization in which the magnetic-field-induced spin magnetization emerges as the only symmetry-allowed linear quantum-geometric response. We demonstrate that this contribution originates entirely from the spin-rotation quantum metric, establishing it as the sole linear quantum-geometric mechanism in such systems. Using representative centrosymmetric altermagnets, including the $d$-wave compound $\mathrm{FeSb}_2$ and the $g$-wave compound $\mathrm{CrSb}$, we show that the spin-rotation quantum metric directly controls this response. Crucially, we predict a giant linear spin magnetization of order $10^{-2}μ_B\,\mathrm{nm}^{-3}$ at magnetic fields of $\sim 10\,\mathrm{mT}$, exceeding typical experimental values for conventional magnets by several orders of magnitude. Our results identify a universal quantum geometric mechanism of spin magnetization operative in centrosymmetric systems in general, and establish centrosymmetric altermagnets as an ideal platform for its experimental detection with potential applications in spintronics.
summary: This paper identifies a new, universal quantum-geometric mechanism for generating massive spin magnetization in centrosymmetric altermagnets. It shows that the spin-rotation quantum metric drives a magnetic-field-induced response that is orders of magnitude larger than conventional magnetic signals, offering a promising platform for spintronics.
main_problem: Identifying the quantum-geometric mechanism responsible for linear spin magnetization in centrosymmetric altermagnets, where inversion and $C_n T$ symmetries suppress conventional responses.
main_result: The authors discover that magnetic-field-induced spin magnetization in centrosymmetric altermagnets originates entirely from the spin-rotation quantum metric and can reach a giant magnitude of $10^{-2} \mu_B 	ext{ nm}^{-3}$ at very low fields.
method: A unified theoretical framework using a generalized quantum geometric tensor that incorporates both momentum translations and spin rotations of Bloch states, combined with symmetry analysis and linear response theory.
model_or_system: Centrosymmetric altermagnets, specifically $d$-wave $	ext{FeSb}_2$ and $g$-wave $	ext{CrSb}$, modeled using a tight-binding Hamiltonian featuring spin-split band structures and Néel exchange fields.
key_observables: Linear spin magnetization induced by an external magnetic field, spin-rotation quantum metric, and spin-rotation Berry curvature.
important_parameters: Magnetic field strength ($\sim 10 	ext{ mT}$), spin-rotation quantum metric ($R_{ab}$), and the predicted magnetization scale of $10^{-2} \mu_B 	ext{ nm}^{-3}$.
main_assumptions: Weak-disorder limit using the relaxation-time approximation; focus on the linear response regime.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the concept of magnetic-field-induced magnetization in altermagnets, showing the vanishing net magnetization at zero field and the prohibition of electric-field-driven response in centrosymmetric systems.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of spin magnetization in altermagnets, develops a generalized quantum geometric tensor framework, applies symmetry constraints to decompose magnetization contributions, and demonstrates the effect using specific material models ($	ext{FeSb}_2$ and $	ext{CrSb}$).
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Magnetic-field-induced spin magnetization in an altermagnet: In the absence of external perturbations (F), an altermagnet exhibits vanishing net spin magnetization despite a spin-split band structure. A finite linear spin mag- netization (MB) is induced under an applied magnetic field (B), whereas the linear response to an electric field (E) is forbidden in a centrosymmetric altermagnet. The magnetic- field-driven response originates from the spin-rotation quan- tum metric.

FIG. 2. Spin magnetization in the d-wave altermagnet FeSb2: (a) Spin-projected band structure along the high- symmetry path Z–Γ–S–R–T, showing pronounced nonrela- tivistic spin splitting along the Γ–S and Z–R directions. The crystal structure is shown in the inset. (b) Spin-projected Fermi surface in the kx–ky plane at µ = 0.16 eV, highlighting the characteristic d-wave anisotropy of the spin-split bands. (c) yy-component of the spin-rotation quantum metric in the Brillouin zone, revealing the underlying geometric structure. (d) yy-component of the magnetic-field-induced linear spin magnetization response tensor as a function of chemical po- tential.

FIG. 3. Spin magnetization in the g-wave altermagnet CrSb: (A) Spin-projected band structure along the high- symmetry path −M ′–Γ–M ′, showing pronounced nonrela- tivistic spin splitting along the given directions. The crystal structure is shown in the inset. (B) Spin-projected Fermi sur- face in the kx–ky plane at µ = 0.16 eV, highlighting the char- acteristic d-wave anisotropy of the spin-split bands. (c) Distri- bution of yy component of the spin-rotation quantum metric across the Brillouin zone, revealing the underlying quantum- geometric mechanism responsible for the response in panel (d). (d) Chemical-potential dependence of the yy component of the linear spin magnetization response...
